customer_comments.customer_comments:
Borrower persona: My name is Daniel Michael Red Elk (DOB 1987-04-19). I am an enrolled member of the Spokane Tribe of Indians and currently live at 412 Cedar Ridge Loop, Wellpinit, WA 99040 with my wife and two children (ages 7 and 10). I have been employed full-time with the Spokane Tribal Utilities Department as a maintenance technician for the past 6 years. My average gross monthly income from employment is about $4,600 before taxes, plus occasional overtime that can add another $200–$400 in busy months (winter plowing, summer repairs). My wife works part-time at the tribal clinic front desk and brings in about $1,200 per month; however, only my income is listed on this statement since I am the primary borrower.

Purpose/context of this statement: I am completing this Personal Financial Statement in connection with an application for a Spokane Tribal Credit short-term loan to consolidate some higher-interest credit card debt and to cover upcoming school expenses for my kids (school clothes, supplies, and sports fees). I have tried to be as detailed and transparent as possible so that the credit office has a clear picture of our household situation.

Home details: We purchased our home in 2019 through the Spokane Tribal Credit Housing Program. The present estimated market value is about $325,000 based on a comparative market analysis that a local realtor did in late 2024, as well as looking at recent sales of similar 3-bedroom homes in the Wellpinit area. The outstanding mortgage balance is approximately $248,000, and our monthly payment of $1,850 includes principal, interest, and escrow for property taxes and insurance. We have never missed or been late on any mortgage payments. The home is a 3-bedroom, 2-bath single-family house, about 1,650 square feet, on tribal trust land. We plan to stay in this house long term as it is close to family and my job.

Insurance details: Our homeowner’s insurance is through a regional insurer that works with the tribal housing program. The coverage amount is set to roughly the replacement cost of the home, which is about $325,000. Our monthly premium is approximately $92, paid through the mortgage escrow account, so we do not pay it separately out-of-pocket. For vehicles, we maintain full coverage on my primary vehicle (2018 Subaru Outback) and liability plus collision on the older Ford F-150. The auto coverage limit of $500,000 for me reflects combined single limit liability coverage, plus comprehensive and collision. My auto insurance premium is about $128 per month, paid on a 6-month policy term that we renew regularly (hence the 6-month term selection). My co-signer, my cousin, carries a separate policy on her own vehicle with a coverage limit of about $300,000 and pays roughly $96 per month.

Vehicles and usage: I own two vehicles:
1) 2018 Subaru Outback Premium AWD – This is our main family vehicle used for commuting to work, taking the kids to school and sports, grocery trips into Spokane, and visiting extended family. It is paid off; I finished paying the auto loan in 2023. The present value of $16,500 is based on Kelley Blue Book estimates for similar mileage and condition. It is in good mechanical shape with about 92,000 miles.
2) 2012 Ford F-150 XLT 4x4 – This is a higher-mileage work truck I use for hauling tools, firewood, and doing side jobs like small home repairs or yard work for relatives and neighbors. It is also paid off. The value of around $8,500 reflects its age, mileage (about 155,000 miles), and some cosmetic wear (rust spots, small dents). It is still reliable and useful but not worth as much as the Subaru.

Co-signer details: My co-signer is my cousin, Angela Rose Two Rivers (DOB 1990-09-08). She lives in Spokane, WA, and works as a medical assistant at a community health clinic. She owns a 2016 Toyota Camry LE with an estimated value of about $10,500. She still has an auto loan on that car with an approximate monthly payment of $265. She also has some modest credit card balances (about $2,100 total) that she pays around $95 per month toward. Angela has a stable work history and steady income, which is why she agreed to co-sign on this loan application to help me secure a better rate.

Existing Spokane Tribal Credit loans: I currently have three active loans with Spokane Tribal Credit:
1) Long-term loan – This was originally for home improvements (roof repair and adding better insulation). The current balance is around $18,250 with a monthly payment of $275. I have been making these payments consistently for the last 3 years without any late payments.
2) Short-term loan – This is a smaller loan I took out to cover a furnace repair and some unexpected medical travel expenses when my father was hospitalized in Seattle. The current balance is about $2,200 with a monthly payment of $190. I have been paying this on time for the past 11 months.
3) Education loan – This loan helped me complete a two-year technical certification program in HVAC and building maintenance at Spokane Community College. The remaining balance is approximately $6,400, and I pay $135 per month. This training directly supported my current job with the tribal utilities department and has increased my earning potential.

Credit cards and other debts: I currently carry around $3,850 in total credit card and small personal debt balances. These are spread across two major credit cards and one store card. My combined minimum monthly payments are about $165, but I have been trying to pay more than the minimum when possible. The primary reason I am applying for this new short-term loan is to consolidate most of these balances into a single payment at a lower interest rate, which should make it easier to pay them down faster and free up some monthly cash flow. My co-signer’s credit card balance of about $2,100 is separate and she continues to manage that on her own.

Child support / alimony: Neither I nor my co-signer have any alimony or child support obligations. I am married, and my children live with us full-time, so there are no support orders in place. Angela is single with no children.

Assets vs. liabilities summary: The total assets listed for me (approximately $350,000) consist mostly of our primary residence and my two vehicles. We do not own any additional real estate, vacation property, or investment accounts beyond small retirement contributions through my employer (not listed here as they are not easily accessible for collateral). My total liabilities (around $339,700) are primarily the home mortgage and the various Spokane Tribal Credit loans and credit card balances. While we do carry a significant mortgage (which is typical for a family home), we have been steadily building equity as property values have risen and as we pay down the principal each month.

Payment history and reliability: I want to emphasize that I have a strong record of making payments on time. Over the last five years, I have not had any late payments on my mortgage or tribal credit loans. There were a couple of times during the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic when money was tight due to reduced work hours, but I proactively contacted the credit office to discuss options and was able to adjust my payment schedule slightly without missing any obligations. Since then, my work hours have been stable, and we have maintained a consistent budget to keep up with all payments.

Budget and ability to repay: After accounting for mortgage, utilities, groceries, gas, insurance, and existing loan payments, our household typically has a small but positive monthly surplus. Consolidating my higher-interest credit card debt into a single short-term tribal loan should reduce our overall monthly required payments and interest costs. For example, instead of making 3–4 separate credit card payments with varying due dates and higher interest rates, I would make one fixed payment to Spokane Tribal Credit, which I find easier to plan around each month. I also occasionally earn a little extra income from side jobs (small home repairs, snow removal, yard work), which I usually apply directly toward debt reduction.

Reason for co-signer: Although my income is stable, my existing debt levels and credit score are not perfect due to some past late payments on store cards from several years ago. Having Angela as a co-signer strengthens the application because she has a solid credit history and lower overall debt. She understands that she is responsible if I default, and we have discussed this in detail. We are both committed to ensuring the loan is repaid on schedule.

Additional context and future plans: Over the next 2–3 years, my goal is to pay off the current short-term loan, the new consolidation loan I am applying for, and reduce my credit card usage significantly. I intend to keep using only one primary credit card for emergencies and small purchases that I can pay off each month. I also plan to continue investing in my skills and certifications in building maintenance and HVAC, which could lead to incremental pay raises or promotion opportunities within the tribal utilities department. Any increases in income will be directed first toward paying down remaining debts and building a modest emergency savings fund so that I am less reliant on credit in the future.

I am providing this extended explanation so that the credit office understands not only the numbers listed in the Assets and Liabilities section, but also the story behind them—how these debts arose, how they are being managed, and what my plan is going forward. If any part of this statement is unclear or if additional documentation (pay stubs, bank statements, or loan statements) is needed, I am willing to provide it promptly.
